author: @truenorthsolarconsulting8888
text: Your individual cap voltage assertion that 2.4Vpc isn't correct assumes that the nominal rating of the battery (48VDC) is the top or "full" rating.   A standard LiFePO4 cell is 3.2Vpc nominal.   16X that rating yields 51.2VDC.   A full cell will measure at 3.4Vpc or 54.4VDC.   Those are still both 48VDC nominal batteries.   2.7VDC x 20 is 54VDC - a 48V battery nonetheless.

author: @toddp7170
text: You need to differentiate Electric Double Layer Capacitors from hybrid supercapacitors.  Your Maxwell cap is an EDLC.  The hybrid supercaps utilize the cathode of a Lithium-Ion Battery (LIB) and the anode from an EDLC.  The result is higher energy density, elimination of thermal runaway risk while retaining much of the EDLC cycle life (approx 20k cycles in a hybrid SC).  As far as the dimensions go, 18650 is a common cell size.  Its a machine that turns out this size, but has no relationship to whether it results in a LIB or hybrid supecap.  Finally, the resulting hybrid supercap, because it is partially like a battery, does have power discharge characteristics of a LIB, particularly an LTO if that is the doping used in the cathode.  Your conjectures are just that, a guess.  Cut them open and you'll still have to have a mass spectrometer to analyze the compoents of the layers inside.  It doesn't look like you're equipped with the tools to make anything more than another guess.

author: @rayfernandez3524
text:
i normally don't respond to this immature type of clips, as I always say there's  two sides to a story, why not let an independent lab do the testing without being bias? There's a few test being done around the world with proof.
My questions I have:
1. Why can you fly in these units, and not Lithium – MSDS (cylindrical cells) available? 
2. Why can you discharge these units to below sub-zero temperatures, without degradation?
3. Why aren’t these units degrading like Lithium, we over 40 MWh or storage installed with little to no degradation?
4. Why can you discharge these units to not just 0% state of charge, but 0 Volts. Leave on a concrete floor for a month and start up again. Lithium cannot do this, why not?
5. Why aren’t these units generating heat when charging and discharging like Lithium?
6. You can charge these units between 1C – 10C, no heat build-up, no thermal runaway, little to no degradation, why can’t you do this with Lithium

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I normally don't respond to these immature type of comments - but Lithium batteries are not one thing - there are many types of lithium battery Lithium Iron phosphate, Lithium Cobalt Oxide, Lithium Manganese Oxide, etc etc... Lithium titanate - the kind used here have all the qualities you mention. I don't take exception with the battery type - i take exception they are sold as capacitors - which they are not. I have a question for you - are you being paid by Kilowatt to post this?

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text: I have seen a lot of LTO batteries being referred to as super capacitors. It's usually because they have a massive discharge rate and have that great cycle capacity. You should be able to get them going. From memory that battery chemistry can be discharged down to 0V and still survive. They are also great in hi and low temps and don't explode when abused. The down side being not having as great a capacity which I think you mentioned.

author: @SinsBird
text: Regular (NMC?) lithium ion cells sometimes come back to life if charged at a very low rate, like 50mA (I know for a fact Nokia phones used to do that, I'm sure some other brands did also even to this day) to something like 3.5V ~ 3.7V. Recovered cells usually have higher self discharge rate probably caused by a damaged separator or something. I remember when charging and discharging a 'recovered' 18650 cell, where I reset the CID with a drill bit, at constant current at some point voltage would start jumping up and down. I haven't investigated it, but I have the charging and discharging curves saved.

author: @otherunicorn
text: Capacitors do not have a voltage of their own - just a maximum they can handle. You would NOT want to run a 2.7V cap at 2.7V. 2.4V would allow an operating margin. The voltage across the capacitors would be determined by the voltage being put onto the bus in the first place.  Of course, if the "capacitors" in question are really batteries, none of what I just said applies.

author: @cayrex
text: It is known that kilowatt labs use LTO in the systems, but like you said the company claimed that they uses "battery supercapacitor type". I think it was in clean energy reviews and also many other people claimed the same about the use of LTO. LTO is good lithium type of battery because is safer that NMC or similar. I like LTO and LFP for some energy storage systems. Years back I make 6kWh LTO based storage system for some people,... and I like the fast charge of the cells.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: as far as I am aware mate there is no battery Supercapacitor type of device on sale - that's just market hype - like Turnigey did with there 'graphene' battery

replies.author: @cayrex
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Right and I know. , But the kilowatt labs trying to convince people that they use supercapacitors or so called battery supercapacitors,.... but anyway mane people busted it already.  For now the closest to so called hybrid supercapacitor will be the lithium ion capacitor,.... but  LIC is still classified as a type of supercapacitor.

author: @ChaseBrown231
text:
I saw only one other person say this, but it is very likely they could be hybrid lithium-supercap modules. They come in through hole 18650 form factor, follow the same voltage curve as LTO, cant be completely discharged much like a battery, and arent quite as light as a cap, If my calculations are correct, those nuggets you've got hold about 2.58Wh per "cell" which would be an awful capacity for even the lowest tier 18650's. If they arent lying about the storage capacity, im a bit inclined to think there may be some truth to their claims. 

Have a look around on the Ali's and you will come across a few models.

replies.author: @foxabilo
replies.text: Then why the 1.7C discharge limit?

replies.author: @ChaseBrown231
replies.text: I'd guess because 6000 watts is alot to push over those small cables and cheap hardware. After all, it was their junk management hardware that failed in the first place.

author: @floridacoder
text:
There needs to be consumer rights laws passed that hold companies accountable for this.  There is a plethora of false advertising on electronics especially,  and batteries even more so.  Companies like Amazon do nothing to protect us from these scams.

I can't believe that between the EU and USA none have cracked down on this.  Or even China, but I suspect China has more bad apples than good apples. 

Buyers can't make informed purchasing decisions if the specs are total fabrications.  Consumers are being ripped off for billions and it needs to stop.

author: @duncyy7471
text: LTO cells have a higher resistance against 0v then the standard li ion 18650. Its not optimal for them, but if the temperature was stable in the 0V time they could be recovered. Just connect them to a bench power supply, limit the current and charge them for say 10min to see if they accept charge. if they do, charge them a other 10min and look if you get any heaters. If everything is ok and no hotspots, charge them fully and let them sit fully charged for some time, standard lithium is 2 weeks, after that measure again. cells that dropped to much voltage are damaged/dead. If you have heaters or voltage dropping cells, the need to be removed (or disconnected) from the pack.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - nice advice

author: @RedPillRachel
text: Please disassemble asap, as there are ways to revive lithium cells which aren't "in the manufacturer's guidance" but work fine anyway. Each cell cuts off *until a boost is applied* and most chargers don't do it. My chinese 18650 charger "XTAR VC8+" advertises that it can do it, and it works, from 0v to 3.2 in one step, and then it charges. The resulting cells have normal capacity and internal resistance, measured with more than one meter, 1 on the XTAR device and a discrete one, so somebody is lying somewhere, and it's not my 2 meters lol!

replies.text: lol - I will get on with tomorrow probably

author: @ancapftw9113
text:
LTO batteries do have a few advantages over standard Li-Ions.  Better cycle life, better discharge/recharge rates, operate down to -40c without being damaged.  But they only have half the capacity of standard Li-ion batteries.

Could be a good find.

author: @Tore_Lund
text: So super capacitors have 1/20 the capacity of Li-ion, yet this box has an energy content comparable to a Li-ion battery module? There are a special kind of hybrid supercapacitors, that both uses electrostatic charge like a capacitor but also has som ion transfer so partially also acts as a battery. So in principle they could have avoided being scammers, if they got some of those instead just soldering 18650 cells in bulk.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @matthewellisor5835
text:
Very disappointing but certainly not unexpected given the general lack of understanding just in cell chemistries, much less when you add in capacitors.
I must add that I had a bit of concern for your safety (no disrespect intended, they just compel from me a great deal of caution) at the video title; a capacitor bank that size could put out more instantaneous than thousands of homes might use. 

My first guess as to the damage is a cascade failure and I hope you'll be able to salvage all but one cell in each series string.
